metadata.summary: Identify required skills and quantities per work package, assess real availability capped at 75-80%, map to activities, and level overloads via delay/split/add/cut options.

content.title: Resource Management
content.summary: Identify required skills and quantities per work package, assess real availability (capped at 75-80% effective capacity), map resources to activities, level overloads via delay/split/add/cut options, and monitor utilization weekly against plan. Resources include human, physical, material, and financial — but human resource planning dominates and must never assume 100% capacity.

why.p: Bottlenecks from missing skills, uneven workload distribution, and schedule delays from unavailability trace back to plans built at 100% utilization that ignore meetings, interruptions, and PTO. Effective capacity for most knowledge workers is 75-80% of nominal hours; plans built above that ceiling always slip on the first sick week or sprint ceremony load.

list.item: Multi-team programs where the same engineers are claimed by 2+ projects
Onboarding or offboarding wave shifting skill mix within 30-60 days
Agency or consulting context billing by utilization
Mid-project resource crisis (key person quit, contractor delayed)
Capacity planning ahead of quarterly OKR commitments
Stable single team under 8 people — informal allocation works; formal plans add overhead
Pure agile teams committed to whole-team ownership — utilization tracking erodes psychological safety
One-week sprint of throwaway research — over-planning waste
Organisations where utilization % is used as a performance metric — creates burnout and gaming

section.name: resource-categories

list.item: Human: team members, contractors. Key considerations: skills, availability, cost, bus factor.
Physical: equipment, facilities. Key considerations: location, capacity, scheduling lead time.
Material: supplies, software licenses. Key considerations: lead time, inventory, renewal cycles.
Financial: budget, cash flow. Key considerations: timing, approval process, EVM reconciliation.

section.p: Human resource planning dominates software and knowledge-work projects. The remaining sections focus on human resources unless otherwise stated.

list.item: Step 1: Identify requirements — for each work package: what skills, how many people, how long, any special constraints.
Step 2: Assess availability — current commitments, effective capacity (hours/week at 75-80%), skills and experience, cost rates.
Step 3: Create resource plan — map resources to activities; produce a resource calendar grid by week and phase.
Step 4: Level resources — resolve overloads: delay tasks (non-critical), split tasks, add resources, reduce scope, or extend timeline.
Step 5: Develop the team — training, mentoring, team building, clear expectations, recognition.
Step 6: Manage the team — track utilization vs. plan weekly; address performance, conflicts, and reassignments.

section.name: effective-capacity-rule

rule.statement: Plan to 75-80% effective capacity, never 100% — meetings, interruptions, and administrative overhead consume at least 20% of nominal hours.
rule.rationale: Plans at 100% utilization collapse on the first sick week or sprint ceremony load, creating cascading delays. The 20% buffer is not slack — it is the cost of doing knowledge work in a real organisation.

section.p: Utilization guidelines by zone: under 50% = underutilized; 50-75% = healthy with buffer; 75-85% = optimal for most roles; 85-100% = full capacity, burnout risk rising; above 100% = unsustainable, address immediately.

list.item: Delay tasks: move non-critical-path tasks to later; no scope or cost impact.
Split tasks: interrupt a task and resume later; useful when work can be paused safely.
Add resources: requires budget and available skill; ramp-up cost is 2 weeks at 50% productivity for contractors.
Reduce scope: cut features; requires sponsor approval and change control.
Extend timeline: delay delivery date; requires sponsor approval and baseline update.

antipattern.description: 100% allocation plans that ignore meetings, email, ceremonies, and breaks.
antipattern.reason: These plans always slip on the first sick week or holiday; they create chronic delivery failures that look like execution problems but are actually planning failures.

rule.statement: Cross-train at least one backup per critical skill; bus factor below 2 is a P1 risk.
rule.rationale: A single-person dependency on a critical skill (database, security, deployment) is a project risk comparable to a high-probability technical risk — it should appear in the risk register.

section.name: agentic-capacity-pipeline

rule.statement: A capacity-planner subagent ingests calendars (PTO, holidays), current Jira/Linear assignments, and skill tags from HR; produces a weekly availability matrix capped at 75% effective capacity.
rule.rationale: Manual capacity planning is always stale; automated ingestion from authoritative sources (calendar + HR + tracker) produces a plan that updates as reality changes.

section.p: A leveler agent compares demand (committed work from WBS) vs. supply (availability matrix), surfaces overload weeks, and proposes options with cost impact. The PM selects; the agent updates the plan and notifies affected leads. Re-runs weekly on cron.

list.item: capacity-planner — pulls PTO, ceremonies, current assignments → effective capacity per person per week (capped at 32h / 80% of 40h standard week).
skill-matcher — matches required skills from WBS to people with skill-level scoring (skill_match × 0.6 + availability × 0.3 + cost_fit × 0.1).
leveler — detects overload weeks, proposes mitigations (delay, split, add, cut scope) with cost and schedule impact per option.
utilization-monitor — weekly delta vs. plan; flags drift greater than 15%; surfaces trend, not snapshot.

example.note: Capacity-planner prompt — hard cap at 80%, explicit subtraction logic:
example.code: You are a capacity-planner. Given calendar events {ical}, sprint commitments {jira_issues}, recurring meetings {meetings}, return per-person weekly hours available for project work. Cap at 32h/week (80% of 40). Subtract: - PTO/holidays (full day = 8h) - Recurring meetings (sum per week) - Reserve 4h/week for interruption buffer Output JSON {person, week, available_h, committed_h, free_h}.

example.note: Skill-matcher prompt — scoring formula prevents gut-feel assignments:
example.code: You are a skill-matcher. Given task {task} requiring {skills} at {level}, return ranked candidates from {team_roster.yaml}. Score = skill_match * 0.6 + availability * 0.3 + cost_fit * 0.1. Reject candidates with score < 0.5. Output JSON with score and rationale.

antipattern.description: Calendar API pulls miss informal meetings, pair sessions, and debugging deep dives.
antipattern.reason: Structured calendar data only captures formally scheduled events; ask the person to confirm their actual available hours before using the raw calendar count for planning.

antipattern.description: Allocation agent packs the calendar to 100% if uncapped.
antipattern.reason: Agents optimize for filling capacity; hard-cap at 80% effective in the prompt and the output schema to prevent burnout plans.
Mid-sprint reassignments break WIP and disrupt flow. Schedule rebalancing only at sprint boundaries.

antipattern.description: Agents read calendar subjects to assess meeting load.
antipattern.reason: Calendar subjects are often sensitive (layoff prep, performance review); redact subject lines before any LLM input, keep only duration and recurrence pattern.

list.item: Skill self-assessments inflate; use last-shipped-feature as ground truth for skill level.
Hourly-rate fields in HR systems mix loaded and unloaded cost; document which rate EVM uses.
Pulling PTO from HR APIs often hits permissioning issues; have a fallback "ask the EM" loop.
Mandatory human checkpoints: resource plan approval, overload escalation to sponsor, contractor engagement, any assignment change that affects the critical path.
gcalcli — read Google Calendar events for capacity (https://github.com/insanum/gcalcli)
khal — CalDAV calendar queries (https://github.com/pimutils/khal)
jira CLI — pull current assignments + estimates (https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli)
linear-cli — Linear capacity/load by user (npm)
tempo (Jira plugin API) — worklog + capacity (https://apidocs.tempo.io)
harvest API — time tracking + capacity (https://help.getharvest.com/api-v2)
clockify API — free time tracking (https://docs.clockify.me/api)
bamboohr API — HR data, PTO, headcount (https://documentation.bamboohr.com)
Float — resource scheduling, REST API; best for agency/consulting contexts (SaaS).
Resource Guru — bookings + leave, REST API; WFH and holiday aware (SaaS).
Forecast.it — AI scheduling, GraphQL; integrates with Harvest (SaaS).
Runn — capacity planning, rich API; supports project + people views (SaaS).
Microsoft Project for Web — Graph API, Azure AD; enterprise default on Microsoft estate (SaaS).
OpenProject — self-host, REST API, Gantt + WBS native; for regulated environments (OSS).
Tempo Planner — Jira-tied, plugin permissioning; useful when already on Jira ecosystem (SaaS).

rule.statement: Track skills with three levels (learning, proficient, mentor) and a last-used date — skill matrices encoded once go stale fast.
rule.rationale: People grow and their skills change; a matrix without last-used dates produces assignments that mismatch actual capability within 6-12 months of authoring.

rule.statement: Resource plan must reconcile with schedule (CPM) and budget (EVM); update all three together when resource changes occur.
rule.rationale: A resource change that does not propagate to schedule and cost creates invisible drift — the plan says on-time but the critical path has already slipped.

rule.statement: Keep a stretch bench — people with 10-20% slack who can absorb spikes without going over 80% effective capacity.
rule.rationale: Without a stretch bench, every unplanned event (sick day, unexpected bug, scope addition) requires a formal escalation; a small buffer keeps velocity stable.

list.item: PMBoK 7th — Team Performance Domain
DeMarco & Lister, "Peopleware" — slack chapter on the cost of full utilization
Allen Ward, "Lean Product and Process Development" — capacity utilization paradox
Float blog, "The 70% rule for resource planning"
Sibling methodologies: schedule-development/, cost-estimation/, earned-value-management/, raci-matrix/, procurement-management/

template.purpose: Compute effective hours per person per week from roster, PTO, and meeting load
Resource plan with summary table, calendar grid, skill matrix, resource risks
Resource request form with role, skills, dates, justification, impact-if-not-filled
